---
title: "R Notebook"
output: html_notebook
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

@inproceedings{johnson-etal-2021-classical, title = "The {C}lassical
{L}anguage {T}oolkit: {A}n {NLP} Framework for Pre-Modern Languages",
author = "Johnson, Kyle P. and Burns, Patrick J. and Stewart, John and
Cook, Todd and Besnier, Cl{'e}ment and Mattingly, William J. B.",
booktitle = "Proceedings of the 59th Annual Meeting of the Association
for Computational Linguistics and the 11th International Joint
Conference on Natural Language Processing: System Demonstrations", month
= aug, year = "2021", address = "Online", publisher = "Association for
Computational Linguistics", url =
"<https://aclanthology.org/2021.acl-demo.3>", doi =
"10.18653/v1/2021.acl-demo.3", pages = "20--29", abstract = "This paper
announces version 1.0 of the Classical Language Toolkit (CLTK), an NLP
framework for pre-modern languages. The vast majority of NLP, its
algorithms and software, is created with assumptions particular to
living languages, thus neglecting certain important characteristics of
largely non-spoken historical languages. Further, scholars of pre-modern
languages often have different goals than those of living-language
researchers. To fill this void, the CLTK adapts ideas from several
leading NLP frameworks to create a novel software architecture that
satisfies the unique needs of pre-modern languages and their
researchers. Its centerpiece is a modular processing pipeline that
balances the competing demands of algorithmic diversity with
pre-configured defaults. The CLTK currently provides pipelines,
including models, for almost 20 languages.", }

This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you
execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code.

Try executing this chunk by clicking the *Run* button within the chunk
or by placing your cursor inside it and pressing *Ctrl+Shift+Enter*.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->

<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxucGxvdChjYXJzKVxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r
plot(cars)
```

<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


*Add a new chunk by clicking the* Insert Chunk\* button on the toolbar
or by pressing *Ctrl+Alt+I*.

When you save the notebook, an HTML file containing the code and output
will be saved alongside it (click the *Preview* button or press
*Ctrl+Shift+K* to preview the HTML file).\*

*The preview shows you a rendered HTML copy of the contents of the
editor. Consequently, unlike* Knit*,* Preview\* does not run any R code
chunks. Instead, the output of the chunk when it was last run in the
editor is displayed.\*

# Data Preparation


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuXG4jIGluc3RhbGwucGFja2FnZXMoXCJmYWN0b2V4dHJhXCIpXG4jIGluc3RhbGwucGFja2FnZXMoXCJkcGx5clwiKVxuIyBpbnN0YWxsLnBhY2thZ2VzKFwiZ2dyZXBlbFwiKVxuXG5saWJyYXJ5KGZhY3RvZXh0cmEpXG5saWJyYXJ5KGRwbHlyKVxubGlicmFyeShnZ3JlcGVsKVxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r

# install.packages("factoextra")
# install.packages("dplyr")
# install.packages("ggrepel")

library(factoextra)
library(dplyr)
library(ggrepel)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


(For the above ggrepel library, see <https://github.com/slowkow/ggrepel>


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
setwd("~/GitHub/cltk-2025-atticus")

dims <- c("title", "author", "path", "form", "lemma", "tag", "Aspect", "Mood", "Number",
                        "Person",
                        "Tense",
                        "VerbForm",
                        "Voice",
                        "Case",
                        "PronType",
                        "Gender",
                        "Polarity",
                        "Degree",
                        "NumType")

data <- read.csv("atticus-study-results.csv", na.strings="", col.names = dims)

summary(data)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->

```
    title              author              path               form          
 Length:5290814     Length:5290814     Length:5290814     Length:5290814    
 Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
                                                                            
                                                                            
                                                                            
                                                                            
    lemma               tag               Aspect              Mood          
 Length:5290814     Length:5290814     Length:5290814     Length:5290814    
 Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
                                                                            
                                                                            
                                                                            
                                                                            
    Number              Person           Tense             VerbForm        
 Length:5290814     Min.   :1.0       Length:5290814     Length:5290814    
 Class :character   1st Qu.:2.0       Class :character   Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Median :3.0       Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
                    Mean   :2.6                                            
                    3rd Qu.:3.0                                            
                    Max.   :3.0                                            
                    NA's   :4377563                                        
    Voice               Case             PronType            Gender         
 Length:5290814     Length:5290814     Length:5290814     Length:5290814    
 Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
                                                                            
                                                                            
                                                                            
                                                                            
   Polarity            Degree            NumType         
 Length:5290814     Length:5290814     Length:5290814    
 Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
                                                         
                                                         
                                                         
                                                         
```



<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->



<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
#Lets get rid of the non-Cicero works now
data <- data[rowSums(data == 'M. Tullius Cicero', na.rm = TRUE) == 0 | data$title == "Letters to Atticus",]

titles <- unique(data$title)
num_words <- table(data$title)
final.frame <- data.frame(title=titles)

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Now that we have the data, what we're *really* interested in is how many
of each tag appears in each work (how many of each POS, how many perfect
verbs, whatever). The point is, we're not interested in every individual
word.

This block of code will loop through and create a new data frame called
***final.frame*** (which is probably poorly named, but I'll fix it later
or I won't). This data frame has a variable for the count of each tag,
and every row is a unique work.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

for (i in 1:length(titles)) {
  frame <- data[data$title == titles[i],]
  frame <- subset(frame, select= c(-form, -path, -NumType, -author, -lemma))
  for (n in 2:length(frame)) {
    t <- table(frame[,n])
    #t <- prop.table(t)
    names <- names(t)
    
    for (name_index in seq(1, length(names))) {
      final.frame[i, names[name_index]] <- unname(t[names[name_index]])
    }
  }
}

#Normalize by number of words in each work
for (i in 2:length(final.frame[1,])) {
  for (n in 1:length(final.frame[,1])) {
    final.frame[n,i] <- final.frame[n,i] * (10000 / num_words[[final.frame[n,]$title]])
  }
}
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


A few things will make our life easier. We don't want any NAs in there,
because that'll gum up the clustering. We'll replace any missing values
with 0, because 0 occurrences of a variable is the only reason there
would be NA values at this stage.

I have a *hopefully* temporary measure where I replace row 46's title
with Apocolocynthosis. The title is given as "Petronius. Seneca
Apocolocynthosis" because it's based off a Loeb edition of that name.
However, when I abbreviate the names at the end of this block, this
makes it ambiguous with the other Perseus text which contains just
Petronius, and **rownames()** doesn't allow duplicates.

I also remove Cicero's works other than the Letters to Atticus. The
reason is that the clustering algorithm greatly prefers to put works by
the same author together. This is great, but we're not interested in the
authorship of the Letters. We're interested in how similar it is to
other texts of the same genre. So, Cicero has to go.

The numbers aren't normalized according to the size of their respective
works yet, so I've divided each number by the total number of words in
the given work in the next chunk. I multiplied each by 100,000 too, just
to get numbers that look a little nicer and aren't in scientific
notation.

Finally, we do what I said above, turning the rownames into the title. I
shorten them to 12 characters max.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r


#Clean up NAs and filter out Cicero's works that are not the Letters to Atticus
final.frame[is.na(final.frame)] <- 0

#Add a number to make

#CAN GET RID OF THIS LATER WHEN I FIX TITLES
#final.frame$title[46] <- "Apocolocyntosis"
#final.frame <- final.frame[grepl("Horace", final.frame$title) == FALSE,]

#cicero_works_nonatt <- c("M. Tulli Ciceronis Orationes", "Librorum de Re Publica Sex", "M. Tulli Ciceronis\nscripta quae manserunt omnia,\nfasc. 43", "de Natura Deorum", "M. Tullii Ciceronis opera quae supersunt omnia", "Tusculanae Disputationes", "M. Tulli Ciceronis Rhetorica, Tomus II", "De Senectute De Amicitia De Divinatione, With An English Translation", "Epistulae ad Familiares", "Letters to and from Quintus")

# #final.frame <- final.frame[(final.frame$title %in% cicero_works_nonatt) == FALSE,]
# 
# 
# #These values give NA, cause issues
# final.frame[c("Neg", "Rcp", "Art")] <- NULL

for (i in 1:length(final.frame$title)) {
  final.frame$title[i] <- paste(as.character(i), final.frame$title[i])
}

rownames(final.frame) <- final.frame$title
final.frame$title <- lapply(X=final.frame$title, FUN=substr, start=1, stop=12)
rownames(final.frame) <- lapply(X=rownames(final.frame), FUN=substr, start=1, stop=12)

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Now that we have a viable DF, let's cut it up a little bit. We don't
want the first column, because that's all text. Not every clustering
algorithm needs the DF to be pre-scaled (that is, subtracting the mean
from every value and dividing it by the standard deviation to make the
comparable). However, some do expect the data to be scaled, so lets
create two variables. **df_clust** isn't scaled, and **df_scaled** is.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuXG5kZl9jbHVzdCA8LSBmaW5hbC5mcmFtZVssMjo1Ml1cbiNkZl9jbHVzdCA8LSBzbGljZV9zYW1wbGUoZGZfY2x1c3QsIG49NDUpXG5cbmRmX3NjYWxlZCA8LSBzY2FsZShkZl9jbHVzdClcbmBgYCJ9 -->

```r

df_clust <- final.frame[,2:52]
#df_clust <- slice_sample(df_clust, n=45)

df_scaled <- scale(df_clust)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


# Analysis

First, to get some of the formatting predefined:


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuZGVuZF90aGVtZSA8LSB0aGVtZShheGlzLnRleHQgPSBlbGVtZW50X3RleHQoc2l6ZT1kZW5kX3NpemUsIHZqdXN0ID0gMSksIG1hcmdpbnMgPSBtYXJnaW4odCA9IG1hcmdpbl9zaXplLCByID0gbWFyZ2luX3NpemUsIGIgPSBtYXJnaW5fc2l6ZSwgbCA9IG1hcmdpbl9zaXplKSlcblxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r
dend_theme <- theme(axis.text = element_text(size=dend_size, vjust = 1), margins = margin(t = margin_size, r = margin_size, b = margin_size, l = margin_size))

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiRXJyb3I6IG9iamVjdCAnZGVuZF9zaXplJyBub3QgZm91bmRcbiJ9 -->

```
Error: object 'dend_size' not found
```



<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Time to cluster! The visualization (which I get with fviz_nbclust below)
shows that 2ish is about as good as we get with clusters (and using hcut
in the next code block, it seems that's a good call).


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

kmeans_basic_cluster <- kmeans(df_scaled, centers=k, nstart=25)
kmeans_basic_table <- data.frame(kmeans_basic_cluster$size, kmeans_basic_cluster$centers)
kmeans_basic_df <- data.frame(Clusters = kmeans_basic_cluster$cluster, df_clust)

#TODO ADD SUMMARIES OF THE ABOVE DATA HERE

#Principal component analysis

pca <- prcomp(df_scaled)

summary(pca)

#Visualizations

fviz_nbclust(df_clust, kmeans, method="wss", nstart=50)

cluster_main <- fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster, 
             data = df_clust, 
             palette = palette, 
             geom = geom,
             ellipse.type = ellipse.type, 
             ggtheme = ggtheme,
             ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)), vjust="inward", hjust="inward", max.overlaps = 12, size=3, )
cluster_main

str(kmeans_basic_cluster)

distance <- get_dist(df_scaled)
fviz_dist(distance, gradient = list(low = "#00AFBB", mid = "white", high = "#FC4E07")) + distance_labels

ggsave(filename = "Images/Distance plot with main d.f.png", dpi="print")

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


But, let's look at the cluster that contains the letters to Atticus.
Maybe a bar chart showing facets of each?


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
att_clust <- kmeans_basic_df$Clusters[rownames(kmeans_basic_df) == "22 Letters t"]

#df.barplot <- data.frame(names = dimnames(kmeans_basic_df)[[2]][-1], attclust = sapply(X=kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters == att_clust,], sum)[-1])

#df.barplot$poetclust <-  sapply(X=kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters == 2,], sum)[-1]

df.barplot <- data.frame(cluster = 1, var = dimnames(kmeans_basic_df)[[2]][-1], clustersum = scale(sapply(X=kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters == 1,], mean)[-1]))
for (i in 2:max(kmeans_basic_df$Clusters)) {
  temp <- data.frame(cluster = i, var = dimnames(kmeans_basic_df)[[2]][-1], clustersum = scale(sapply(X=kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters == i,], mean)[-1]))
  df.barplot <- rbind(df.barplot, temp)
}


ggplot(data = df.barplot, aes(var, clustersum)) + labs(title = "Z-scores of mean number of tags per cluster category") + facet_wrap(~cluster) + geom_col() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust=1, size=4))

ggsave(filename = "./Images/cluster_vals.png", dpi="print")
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


2/15/2026: Interesting to note that all POS's tagged as Singular are
higher in Cluster 3, which Att. belongs to. Verbs also look farthest
above the mean in the same cluster, so that is worth investigating as
well. Present tense is by far the most common in Cluster 3, and it only
comes close in cluster 5.

-   Cluster 3 contains only comedy, Sulpicia, and the Letters to Atticus

-   

But it might be a good idea to look at other methods of clustering as
well. The graphs above seem to show that K-means gets some well-defined
groupings, but let's test out hcut. I do know that there are many
methods of hclust, so the next step after some more experimentation is
to try that.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuXG5kZl9jbHVzdDIgPC0gZGZfY2x1c3Rbc2FtcGxlKG5yb3coZGZfY2x1c3QpLCA4MCksXVxuXG5oY3V0X3Jlc3VsdHMgPC0gaGN1dChkZl9jbHVzdDIpXG5cbmZ2aXpfbmJjbHVzdChkZl9jbHVzdDIsIGhjdXQsIG1ldGhvZD1cIndzc1wiKVxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r

df_clust2 <- df_clust[sample(nrow(df_clust), 80),]

hcut_results <- hcut(df_clust2)

fviz_nbclust(df_clust2, hcut, method="wss")
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuZnZpel9uYmNsdXN0KGRmX2NsdXN0MiwgaGN1dCwgbWV0aG9kID0gXCJzaWxob3VldHRlXCIpXG5gYGAifQ== -->

```r
fviz_nbclust(df_clust2, hcut, method = "silhouette")
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuZnZpel9uYmNsdXN0KGRmX2NsdXN0MiwgaGN1dCwgbWV0aG9kPVwiZ2FwX3N0YXRcIilcbmBgYCJ9 -->

```r
fviz_nbclust(df_clust2, hcut, method="gap_stat")
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->

```
Clustering k = 1,2,..., K.max (= 10): .. done
Bootstrapping, b = 1,2,..., B (= 100)  [one "." per sample]:
.................................................. 50 
.................................................. 100 
```



<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
fviz_dend(hcut_results, palette = palette, 
          geom = geom,
          ellipse.type = ellipse.type,
          type = "rectangle",
          rect=TRUE,
          #horiz=TRUE,
          ggtheme = theme_bw()
) + geom_point() + coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-2000, 10000)) + dend_theme

ggsave(filename = "./Images/cluster_all.png", dpi="print")

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->



<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->



<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbWzEsIlx1MDAxYkcyO1x1MDAxYkgyO1dhcm5pbmdcdTAwMWJoOiBnZ3JlcGVsOiAzMyB1bmxhYmVsZWQgZGF0YSBwb2ludHMgKHRvbyBtYW55IG92ZXJsYXBzKS4gQ29uc2lkZXIgaW5jcmVhc2luZyBtYXgub3ZlcmxhcHNcdTAwMWJnXG4iXV19 -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Note in the following how the Letters to Atticus are quite far away from
Comedy and Petronius! Unlike the previous, when looking at POS tags.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuXG5kZl92ZXJiIDwtIGRmX2NsdXN0WywzNDo0OV1cblxuaGN1dF9yZXN1bHRzIDwtIGhjdXQoZGZfdmVyYilcblxuZnZpel9uYmNsdXN0KGRmX3ZlcmIsIGhjdXQsIG1ldGhvZD1cIndzc1wiKVxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r

df_verb <- df_clust[,34:49]

hcut_results <- hcut(df_verb)

fviz_nbclust(df_verb, hcut, method="wss")
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

fviz_dend(hcut_results, palette = palette, 
          geom = "point",
          ellipse.type = "convex",
          type = "rectangle",
          #horiz=TRUE,
          ggtheme = theme_bw(),
) + geom_point()# + coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-1000, 2100)) + theme(margins = margin(t = margin_size, r = margin_size, b = margin_size, l = margin_size))
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->



<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
scaled_verb <- scale(df_verb)
kmeans_basic_cluster <- kmeans(scaled_verb, centers=5, nstart=20)
kmeans_basic_table <- data.frame(kmeans_basic_cluster$size, kmeans_basic_cluster$centers)
kmeans_basic_df <- data.frame(Clusters = kmeans_basic_cluster$cluster, df_verb)

#TODO ADD SUMMARIES OF THE ABOVE DATA HERE

pca <- prcomp(scaled_verb)

summary(pca)

#Visualizations

choose <- NULL

fviz_nbclust(scaled_verb, kmeans, method="wss", nstart=50)

fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster, choose.vars = choose, data = df_verb, palette = palette, 
             geom = "point",
             ellipse.type = "convex", 
             ggtheme = theme_bw(),
             ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)))
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


When looking at Case, it seems that Comedy, Seneca, Petronius, and the
Letters to Atticus cluster together again. It is a strong enough cluster
that they nearly form their own category.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuXG5kZl9jYXNlIDwtIGRmX2NsdXN0WywxNjoyMl1cblxuaGN1dF9yZXN1bHRzIDwtIGhjdXQoZGZfY2FzZSlcblxuZnZpel9uYmNsdXN0KGRmX2Nhc2UsIGhjdXQsIG1ldGhvZD1cIndzc1wiKVxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r

df_case <- df_clust[,16:22]

hcut_results <- hcut(df_case)

fviz_nbclust(df_case, hcut, method="wss")
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

fviz_dend(hcut_results, palette = palette, 
          geom = "point",
          ellipse.type = "convex",
          type = "rectangle",
          #horiz=TRUE,
          ggtheme = theme_bw(),
) + geom_point()# + coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-1000, 2100)) + theme(margins = margin(t = margin_size, r = margin_size, b = margin_size, l = margin_size))
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->



<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
scaled_case <- scale(df_case)
kmeans_basic_cluster <- kmeans(scaled_case, centers=6, nstart=20)
kmeans_basic_table <- data.frame(kmeans_basic_cluster$size, kmeans_basic_cluster$centers)
kmeans_basic_df <- data.frame(Clusters = kmeans_basic_cluster$cluster, df_case)

#TODO ADD SUMMARIES OF THE ABOVE DATA HERE

pca <- prcomp(scaled_case)

summary(pca)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->

```
Importance of components:
                          PC1    PC2    PC3    PC4     PC5     PC6     PC7
Standard deviation     1.6004 1.2340 0.9630 0.8696 0.82076 0.56416 0.49026
Proportion of Variance 0.3659 0.2175 0.1325 0.1080 0.09623 0.04547 0.03434
Cumulative Proportion  0.3659 0.5835 0.7159 0.8240 0.92020 0.96566 1.00000
```



<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuI1Zpc3VhbGl6YXRpb25zXG5cbmNob29zZSA8LSBOVUxMXG5cbmZ2aXpfbmJjbHVzdChzY2FsZWRfY2FzZSwga21lYW5zLCBtZXRob2Q9XCJ3c3NcIiwgbnN0YXJ0PTUwKVxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r
#Visualizations

choose <- NULL

fviz_nbclust(scaled_case, kmeans, method="wss", nstart=50)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster, choose.vars = choose, data = df_case, palette = c(palette, "brown"), 
             geom = "point",
             ellipse.type = "convex", 
             ggtheme = theme_bw(),
             ) + geom_text(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)))
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


# Misc Tests

I want to understand K-means clustering better, so I'm gonna play around
with it here


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

frame <- data.frame(a=rnorm(10, mean = 10), b=rnorm(10, mean = 20))

scaled <- scale(frame)

scaled

km_result <- kmeans(scaled, centers = 2, nstart = 5)
km_table <- data.frame(km_result$cluster, km_result$centers)

fviz_cluster(km_result, data = scaled, palette = c("gray", "red"), 
             geom = "point",
             ellipse.type = "convex", 
             ggtheme = theme_bw(),
             ) 
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


And some ggplot2 experiments based on the book. Here's some of my
playing around with ch. 8.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
df <- data.frame(x = 1, y = 3:1, family = c("sans", "serif", "mono"))

df

ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) + 
  geom_text(aes(label = family, family = family))

fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster, data = df_clust, palette = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red","gray"), 
             geom = "point",
             ellipse.type = "convex", 
             ggtheme = theme_bw(),
             ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)), vjust="inward", hjust="inward", check_overlap = TRUE, size=3)

fviz_dend(hcut_results, palette = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red"), 
          geom = "point",
          ellipse.type = "convex",
          type = "rectangle",
          #type = "phylogenic",
          #repel = TRUE,
          rect = TRUE,
          cex = 0.4,
          ggtheme = theme_bw()
)

fviz_dend(hcut_results, k=4, palette = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red"), 
          ellipse.type = "convex",
          type = "phylogenic",
          repel = TRUE,
          cex = 0.7,
          ggtheme = theme_bw()
)

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Some crucial notes on the previous section:

-   Adding the check_overlap argument to geom_text() in fviz_cluster()
    is very useful. It removes overlapping labels, privileging those
    that are higher in the data frame (meaning the letters to Atticus
    are usually visible)

-   Reduce cex to 0.7 for readability

## How K-Means Clustering Works

K-Means clustering itself is relatively simple. The computer has a set
of data, and it wants to determine which observations are closer to each
other than another.

Let's first imagine we're talking about actual distance

| Point | X Coordinate | Y Coordinate |
|-------|--------------|--------------|
| A     | 1            | 0            |
| B     | 2            | 0            |
| C     | 1            | 10           |
| D     | 2            | 10           |
| E     | 3            | 10           |

Let's graph these points:


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuXG5kZiA8LSBkYXRhLmZyYW1lKFguQ29vcmRpbmF0ZSA9IGMoMSwyLDEsMiwzKSwgWS5Db29yZGluYXRlID0gYygwLCAwLCAxMCwgMTAsIDEwKSwgcm93Lm5hbWVzID0gTEVUVEVSU1sxOjVdKVxuXG5nZ3Bsb3QoZGF0YSA9IGRmLCBhZXMoeCA9IFguQ29vcmRpbmF0ZSwgeSA9IFkuQ29vcmRpbmF0ZSkpICsgZ2VvbV9wb2ludCgpXG5gYGAifQ== -->

```r

df <- data.frame(X.Coordinate = c(1,2,1,2,3), Y.Coordinate = c(0, 0, 10, 10, 10), row.names = LETTERS[1:5])

ggplot(data = df, aes(x = X.Coordinate, y = Y.Coordinate)) + geom_point()
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Okay, so we want the computer to recognize that there are two groups of
points close together, the ones at the top and the bottom.

The K-means algorithm lets the computer recognize there are two
clusters.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxua20gPC0ga21lYW5zKGRmLCBjZW50ZXJzID0gMiwgbnN0YXJ0ID0gNSlcblxuZnZpel9jbHVzdGVyKGttLCBkYXRhID0gZGYpXG5gYGAifQ== -->

```r
km <- kmeans(df, centers = 2, nstart = 5)

fviz_cluster(km, data = df)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


But we're not looking at distances, we're looking at data. However, we
can apply the same method to data that doesn't represent an actual
distance.

Let's say we have a bunch of students, and each take two classes. We
want to sort the students into groups, but how do we do that when they
have two grades in different classes? We could do it by eye, but what if
they were taking 4 classes? 5 classes? How can we objectively sort them
into groups?

Starting with the simplest case, here are some grades:

| Student | English | Math |
|---------|---------|------|
| Sally   | 95      | 89   |
| Susan   | 96      | 72   |
| Sam     | 55      | 78   |
| Shelly  | 76      | 75   |
| Stu     | 40      | 96   |

We can plot these values in space, even though they are


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

df <- data.frame(English = c(95, 96, 55, 76, 40), Math = c(89, 72, 78, 75, 96), row.names = c("Sally", "Susan", "Sam", "Shelly", "Stu"))

ggplot(df, aes(x = English, y = Math)) + geom_point() + geom_text(aes(label = rownames(df)), nudge_y = -2)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Well, let's do the same clustering algorithm again.

NOTE: I tried it again without scaling, the results are similar, but
different. Now, Stu is matched up with Sam. (The eagle-eyed among you
might notice there's an extra step in here. If you're curious, we needed
to scale the values (subtract the mean and divide by the standard
deviation) so that they're comparable with each other.)


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuXG4jc2NhbGVkX2RmID0gc2NhbGUoZGYpXG5cbmttIDwtIGttZWFucyhkZiwgY2VudGVycyA9IDIsIG5zdGFydCA9IDIpXG5cbmZ2aXpfY2x1c3RlcihrbSwgZGF0YSA9IGRmKVxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r

#scaled_df = scale(df)

km <- kmeans(df, centers = 2, nstart = 2)

fviz_cluster(km, data = df)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Notice how the other students are similar to each other because they're
scores in their two classes are pretty close together. Stu, however, has
one high and one low value (he really doesn't like English), so he's
very different from the others.

R is great because it also handles data with more than two dimensions.
If each student had many classes (Math, English, History, Science,
Music, Art), then we could use principal component analysis to plot
differences between these datasets in 2D space. That way, K-means works
even for large datasets.

<!-- rnb-text-end -->
